reasoning: To reach 8+ Fed rate cuts (equaling 200+ bps) in 2025, the U.S. economy would likely need to enter a severe recession or experience a major disinflationary shock. Following the 2024 U.S. elections, market expectations have priced in rising inflation risks due to expected tariffs and deficit spending, causing traders to slash 2025 rate cut expectations to just 2-3 cuts (50-75 bps). The FOMC’s most recent dot plot projects only four 25-bps cuts in 2025. Without a recession, a steady cadence of 8 cuts over 8 meetings is extremely improbable. While historically the Fed cuts by 200+ bps during recessions (which happen roughly ~12-15% of the time per year), the risk of stagflation limits the Fed's ability to aggressively ease even in a mild downturn. Therefore, giving weight to the baseline recession risk but accounting for current macroeconomic crosscurrents, I estimate the probability around 9%.
